text: This study frames attack detection within the smart grid as a statistical learning challenge, addressing various attack scenarios across both batch and online measurement settings. We propose a detection framework that utilizes machine learning algorithms to classify measurements as either secure or compromised. By leveraging prior system knowledge, this approach effectively overcomes data constraints imposed by the sparse structure of grid networks. To model the detection process, we employ a suite of supervised and semi-supervised learning algorithms integrated with decision- and feature-level fusion. Furthermore, we analyze the relationship between the statistical and geometric properties of attack vectors to demonstrate how learning-based methods can identify "unobservable" attacks that typically bypass conventional checks. Extensive testing on IEEE standard systems indicates that the proposed machine learning framework consistently outperforms traditional state-vector estimation methods in terms of detection accuracy and reliability.
